item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article itself is kinda meh, but that monologue at the end is one of the most strangely chilling things I've ever read on this site, and turns an otherwise average article great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The A██████ Hotel has been purchased and is no more as an independent business. Life imitates art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reviews tho (especially the 2nd)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That monologue. Beautiful. The premise of people turning into hotels is completely insane. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just finished reading it and am still a bit undecided, but overall I kind of liked it, and, while the ennui of the monologue didn't quite get me, I'm a sucker for long monologues, so what can I say.

There are a couple of things that I thought were a bit weird, and couldn't get them out of my head, the first is more of a form nitpick, the second is more related to the concept:

+ show block
– hide block

- Why isn't the television in the room labeled 2432-2? We already enstabilished that the growht is 2432-1 and that it works only on that specific tv, so why isn't the tv labeled, but only referenced as "the tv inside 2432"?

- At first I thought the subjects were transforming into hotel rooms, and I already thought that was weird, then I read the author comment where he says they're transforming into whole hotels and that *really* broke my suspension of disbelief.
I mean, I really liked the reference to reproductive cycles of cicadas, but I found the thought of an hotel sprouting like a mushroom where the poor fella decides to collapse on the ground really funny, not to mention if someone brings the cadaver into a morgue, leaves for the night and the next day *poof*, here's an hotel inside the morgue?
To me the ending of the skip would gain a bit more of a kick if the transformation were more subtle, something like a bit of the mind control comes back, the subject is compelled to sign in another hotel, maybe far from home (the fascination toward traveling is already referenced in the skip), and in the room turns into another anomalous tv-with-a-growth, or, hell, even a brand new room would be belivable at that point (after all it's mentioned that room 710 didn't exist before).

PS: It bears mentioning that I'm quite new to the site, I've only been lurking heavily for less than a year and this article has been on the site for quite some time, so if I'm missing something, or if my concerns have been addressed in a tale or another skip, please tell me, I'd be happy to know more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thought this would just be a normal weird room SCP but the bit about people getting turned into copies of SCP-2432 really creeped me out.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"give your cat the last of his food and some water"

That was the line that irrationally pissed me off the way "These people all love their jobs" pissed off Z. I can't imagine fucking off into the great wide open forever and leaving my cats to fend for themselves or starve. I'm traveling myself at the moment, and every third thought that comes into my head is whether my cats are all right and still being taken care of. I couldn't abandon them any more than I could walk out on my family without a word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have translate this into Chinese.

Absolutely nice,upvote and hope you are better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation should do some testing on hotel employees. I can guarantee that all the built-up frustration we get working at these places would overwrite the room's memetic effects. Our reviews would just be a long string of swear words in all-caps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, that's incredible! That was definetely intended to be that way, making hotel workers angry. I really appreciate comments like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And the staff, also all so attentive in their smart little uniforms. Don’t listen to the naysayers. These people all love their jobs. You know they do.

I read this as I stand, semi-attentive at the front (standing) desk of a hotel while a gaggle of old people talk about old people things four meters to my left and "Come on Irene" plays on the loudspeakers from the ceiling. It's already dark and there are still 8 check-ins left before I can roll the night for both the hotel and built-in restaurant, the latter of which of which I need to add each individual receipt manually for each credit card, and cash.

I have never felt such visceral hatred for any fiction than I have for whatever the hell intelligence is in control of this SCP.

That out of the way

Pretty good article, though I thought the bit with the body mutating in to a hotel room to be unnecessary. It felt like it was tacked on just to make it scarier, with the problem being that your body turning in to a hotel room not really being scary so much as weird and silly. Still, I liked it enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I put on the song as I read the monologue. Absolutely lovely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I suddenly want to go to a hotel

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bears are happy with these changes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this an alternate reality where SCP-2875 wasn't contained?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This bears repeating: I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happy April Fools Day!

GRAAAAGH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That monologue hit so close to home. That alone might have unsettled me more than anything else on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The reviews are a wonderful mixture of silly and disturbing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember helping with this back in the early stages.
What a payout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My bad, I was going on outdated information. It wasn't a criticism per se (though I admit it came of as such), I just commented because the author upvoted the work quite recently, so I thought it done in a sneaky fashion. Apologies if I came of as brusque or accusatory (this is also directed at LordStonefish, btw).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Self up voting isn't against the rules anymore. You'll have to find something else to criticize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's with the sneaky self-upvote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I should probably clarify a little. I'm not necessarily advocating for you specifically to move into another style of writing or to address the larger site wide trend issues yourself, though if that's the way you want to proceed, more power to you.

This is probably equal parts explanation of my vote and discussion of possible areas in which things might have been done differently. It's also important to remember that the community has seen fit to keep this article around, so there's definitely something of value here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks Kalinin.

I'll try to make future articles more accessible to diverse audiences. I personally am not a college age bachelor, but I wanted to freak out that specific crowd.

As for the broken english, that part of the draft was written months ago, back when I guess it stil had said some novelty. The more I think about it, the more I realize this article is catered exclusively to that 18-24 year old dude: the body horror, the reviews, the monologue's audience. What essentially ended up happening is this became an attack on reddit users sandwiched between cliche (if apparently liked) site horror tropes.

I'm glad people liked it, but 10 days on I realize I can do much better.

Thanks so much for the critique. Very helpful. I aspire to write the more literary skips you make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ couple of nitpicks first
- hide text

Safe Euclid

This is a specific issue of mine. I don't like strikethrough text in what is supposedly an electronic document. I would imagine they would have just revised the entry in the database and been done with it. Same goes for the outdated containment section. I also don't care for how strikeout text uglifies a page. But that's not something that really affects my vote.

SCP-2432 is Room 70 of the A██████ Hotel in State College, Pennsylvania

This is part of my crusade on this specific issue. But it makes no sense whatsoever to redact in this manner. As the comments demonstrated, a five second google search will give you the information, so why bother concealing it? Either we're cleared to know, or we're not. Write it accordingly.

My first issue with this is the compulsive nature of it. It's been explained in great detail elsewhere, but I don't usually like these kinds of stories because it turns people into mindless zombies, and there's not a whole lot of interesting implications from "thing what turn you into a mindless zombie." If something is going to affect someone's behavior, I want it to do so because the cause makes sense and the way it affects people is consistent with how people normally act. I find that it resonates more that way.

I don't care for the broken English reviews and the fact that it's kind of becoming a cliché at this point. That's less your specific sin and more the fact that it's been done enough times (myself included) now that I'm pretty tired of it. The purpose of the reviews is to sort of increase the creep factor and establish that this is a contagion, but the former is undercut by the well-worn nature of the narrative device, and the latter is accomplished by the ending.

The monologue, which takes up a good part of this, just doesn't grab me. It's not that it's poorly written, but as a man older than a large portion of the site's demographics, the ennui of bachelorhood is a) not immediately relevant to me, and thus not nearly as affecting as it would be on most of our readers, and b) orders of magnitude less soul-wrenching than the shit you're going to encounter later. Part of this is just Old Man Kalinin imposing his personal taste, but this also feeds into the fact that we should be expanding our point of view to places beyond 18-24 year old dude on the internet. For all of the jawing and agitation about social justice and diversity that goes on around the periphery of this community, we have an extremely deep rut where most of our narrators and in-universe writers appear to be fresh out of college American males. That's a large issue to hang on a single article, and likely somewhat unfair, but while this section of the article worked well for a lot of readers, it fell flat for me, and that does affect my vote, given its place of prominence.

As for the rest, I'm not mad at the body horror elements of this, but I've seen it before in Videodrome. Chalk it up to being jaded. It's not a bad article by any stretch, but there was no single portion of it that really grabbed me and resonated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let me put it this way. No supernatural phenomena exist or can exist (>99%). The person who wrote this monologue is a regular human who breathes oxygen (>99%).

I'm disturbed and discomforted by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ominous broken english was effective 'cruise control for horror', so to speak, the first two hundred times it was used. As of late, I've had trouble giving half a damn.

I quite like the central conceit and the majority of the article, though. The roomnews monologue in particular was excellent, and I'd recommend that people interested in writing good monologues read it and try to assess what makes it work.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well it's not a person advertising per se.

Not to mess with ambiguity or headcanons, but this a look into, as WrongJohnSilver put it in chat, the mind of something deeply alien, phrased in extremely relatable terms. Personally I haven't made up my mind if it is even alive/sapient at all.

If I had to describe the mindset of the entity responsible at all, then think The Southern Sombrero Cacti meet Cousin Johnny.

item.title: Re: Object class
item.content:encoded:
I fixed, at your chat suggestion. Added a paragraph about the foundation buying the hotel, changed object class and added in a "possible upgrade" line to mollify the people who liked it as a Keter.

Hopefully everyone should be pleased. Thanks Voct! You've really helped it make more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
13% of subjects who viewed the monologue under controlled conditions describe feeling mildly disturbed or discomforted

It's a person advertising a hotel by picking at the deep-seated self-loathing and crushed dreams billions have, with some very odd word choices.

I can make thousands uncomfortable by posting it on reddit.

Does not sound anomalous to me, or worth mentioning, unless they control for it somehow.

item.title: Object class
item.content:encoded:
Why is this Keter? Containment is simple.

Purchase the hotel; if need be, lean on the owners until they sell. Close the hotel.

Voila, SCP can no longer activate. Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an extremely underrated movie, and feels very SCP-ish to me, as do a lot of Cronenberg's films.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had Videodrome in mind when thinking up the disease, as is obvious. Thanks for the comparison to a master!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. Very Cronenbergian; I can totally see this being the plot of the one of his films if it was expanded upon. The transformation those "infected" go through as well as the pustule on the back of the television remind me a lot of his brand of body horror, especially the stuff from Videodrome. The room itself kind of reminds me of Stephen King's 1408 as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I remember correctly, the rooms have the standard three digit floor-first numbering scheme, so no Room 70. But I barely care.

(And when I see the phrase "favorite State College meat," I think Herwig's.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy crap, this was great. I could have kept reading more and more of those reviews. I particularly enjoyed "some of my favorite State College meat". Excellent monologue, and things sure took a icky turn there at the end. Well done, this. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha look at that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) The reviews are pretty great.

2) The roomnews monologue was too me irl for me irl.

3) WHAT THE FUCK WHAT THE FUCK

No uncollapsed images on the forums, please. -Zyn
hide

WHAT THE FUCK WHAT THE FUCK how the FUCK HOW WHA THE FU CKJ

I was going to open the page history to look for code that catches the last lastfm playback, or something. what the fuck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember looking at this in chat and absolutely loving! Great work, take my upvote. Nice combo of body horror and incoherent creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah. I was not expecting the reviews to be creepy. And I was definitely not expecting that keter hook.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What brought you to my backwater town. When was this? Was there a room 70?

Weird to think one of my favorite writers on the site was so near to me and I didn't know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The real draw is those reviews. So unsettling! The monologue is itself a little on the unsettling side, but might also be too long. Still, you've got quite a few effects going on here — the reviews, the TV cancer, the disease — and they all meld together well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I've stayed at the A██████ Hotel. Interesting choice.

Second, incredible monologue.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved every bit of it; From the description to the creepyness of the monologue and infected people, I really loved this! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this. I think this is the best new article I've read this month.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The infected continue to transform after death. They become hotels after they die.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So on that date they will either become hotels or die? Why did the infected D-Class die?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well the first example is less a meme than just straight up mind control.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reviews are good, I like roomnews and the monologue, the disease is nicely squicky. But for me they seem like three separate items.

There seem to be two memetic effects - one to make people write the reviews, and one in the reviews themselves. Does there need to be both?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My husband and my husband and I have walked drooling path to get here.

Hmmm. HMMMMMM.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are two dates mentioned in the reviews, twenty years and a few days apart. As we get closer to the second date, more who wrote reviews began to transform.

The room's breeding process is similar to a cicada, the new generation hibernates a long time before the population explodes out and begins the process anew.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that went down a rabbit hole…
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what the importance of the date 1/8/19 is. Shouldn't these guys be quarantined indefinitely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great stuff, very solid. And that Keter hook comes (almost) out of nowhere. One of the easiest upvotes I've given in weeks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Relax said the night man/We are programmed to receive./ You can check-out any time you like/But you can never leave!

Special thanks to Quin, LadyKatie, AidenEldritch, Levi, DolphinSlugchugger and more for critique.

All images by me.
